narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile shows determination to pursue this path despite coming from a lower-middle income family background. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 73.8% chance that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed early. This prediction hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and significantly pushed the model toward forecasting bar failure, since stronger high school performance in later years often correlates with bar success. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 26, well below the typical 36.7 average, which further tilted the prediction against you by signaling potential challenges in the rigorous legal reasoning needed for the bar. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90—slightly under the 3.21 average—worked in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting a pass and somewhat offsetting the other concerns. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your academic deciles were notably lower than the averages around 5.5, making these gaps stand out as the biggest hurdles in your case. While factors like your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of applicants, they didn't sway the outcome much. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment focused on bar passage patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, as that could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your later academic performance through targeted prep or additional coursework might also help elevate those decile rankings. Many applicants in similar situations reapply successfully after addressing key weaknesses—your story isn't over, and with focused effort, you can build a stronger case next time.
